edges.justification: Real yields represent the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding gold; when real yields rise, TIPS offer a guaranteed positive real return that competes directly with gold, reducing gold's attractiveness and price.
The Fed Funds rate raises the return on cash and money-market instruments relative to gold, affecting gold's opportunity cost and attractiveness as a non-yielding asset.
Rising market-implied inflation expectations increase demand for gold as an inflation hedge, directly supporting higher gold prices through the expectations channel.
Realized CPI inflation represents actual purchasing-power erosion, triggering inflation-hedge demand for gold as investors seek to preserve real wealth.

Gold is priced in U.S. dollars globally; a stronger dollar makes gold more expensive in other currencies, reducing global demand and depressing the dollar-denominated gold price, while a weaker dollar has the opposite effect.
Gold and equities exhibit a risk-on/risk-off relationship: positive equity returns signal risk appetite and reduce safe-haven demand for gold, while equity sell-offs drive capital rotation into gold.
Elevated VIX signals financial market stress and heightened risk aversion, driving safe-haven capital flows into gold and supporting higher gold prices.
Widening credit spreads indicate systemic financial or corporate stress, prompting flight-to-safety demand for gold as investors seek assets uncorrelated with the corporate debt system.

Rising government debt-to-GDP raises concerns about fiscal sustainability, currency debasement risk, and financial repression, driving demand for gold as a hedge against sovereign credit deterioration.
Oil prices affect gold through the cost channel (energy is a major input to gold mining) and the inflation-expectations channel (higher oil feeds into CPI and inflation-hedge demand for gold).

The global business cycle captured by the CLI drives rotations between growth-sensitive assets and defensive safe-haven assets like gold; below-trend global activity increases gold's safe-haven demand.
Mine production is the primary supply-side flow into gold markets; higher production increases available supply and exerts downward pressure on price, holding demand constant.
Central bank net purchases represent official-sector physical demand for gold; net buying adds to total demand and supports higher prices, while net selling pressures prices downward.
Gold ETF net inflows require physical gold to be purchased and vaulted, directly increasing demand and removing supply from the market, while outflows release physical gold and pressure prices lower.

Jewelry is the largest category of physical gold demand; higher jewelry consumption adds to total demand supporting prices, and conversely high gold prices reduce jewelry purchases in price-sensitive consumer markets.
Elevated geopolitical risk triggers flight-to-safety demand for gold as the primary tangible safe-haven asset, as investors and central banks seek assets with no counterparty or jurisdictional risk.
High economic policy uncertainty raises the risk premium on policy-exposed assets and drives demand for gold, which has no counterparty risk and no direct exposure to any single policy regime.
Consumer pessimism about multi-year economic conditions increases retail demand for defensive stores of value including physical gold and gold ETFs, supporting higher gold prices.

Silver and gold share overlapping inflation-hedge and safe-haven demand drivers; price movements in one precious metal tend to co-move with the other through shared investor demand channels and portfolio substitution effects.

Rising unemployment signals economic weakness, triggers monetary easing, and raises recession risk, all of which increase safe-haven demand for gold through the monetary-policy and risk-aversion channels simultaneously.
The Fed Funds rate anchors the short end of the yield curve and influences longer-term real yields through the expectations channel and the term structure of interest rates.

Monetary policy tightening via higher Fed Funds rates signals the Fed's commitment to controlling inflation, which directly suppresses market-implied long-term inflation expectations embedded in break-even rates.

Real yields and break-even inflation are arithmetic components of the nominal Treasury yield (nominal = real + break-even); changes in one component mechanically affect the relationship between the others when nominal yields adjust.
Realized CPI inflation updates investors' priors about future inflation, directly influencing the level of market-implied break-even inflation expectations embedded in TIPS pricing.
The Fed sets the Fed Funds rate in direct response to realized inflation under its price-stability mandate; higher CPI inflation mechanically triggers monetary tightening.
Under the Fed's dual mandate, rising unemployment signals labor-market weakness that prompts monetary easing (lower Fed Funds), while falling unemployment toward full employment supports tightening.
Higher U.S. policy rates increase the relative return on dollar-denominated assets, attracting capital inflows and strengthening the dollar index.
Higher U.S. real yields make dollar-denominated safe assets more attractive relative to foreign alternatives, drawing capital into the dollar and strengthening DXY.
During risk-off episodes when VIX spikes, investors flight-to-safety into U.S. dollar assets, typically strengthening the dollar index as a reserve-currency safe haven.
Credit spreads and equity volatility both reflect financial market stress and are driven by common underlying risk-aversion shocks; stress episodes simultaneously widen credit spreads and spike VIX.
Large negative equity returns mechanically increase implied volatility and fear, driving VIX higher; the leverage effect and investor panic during drawdowns directly drive the VIX level.
Equity market downturns signal deteriorating corporate health and raise default risk perceptions, causing credit spreads to widen as investors demand greater compensation for holding corporate debt.

The global CLI is a leading indicator of economic activity; improving global economic conditions support corporate earnings expectations and equity returns, while deteriorating CLI signals contraction and weighs on equities.
A weakening global leading indicator raises recession probabilities and default risk perceptions, causing credit spreads to widen as markets price in higher corporate distress.
U.S. labor market conditions are a key component of economic cycle assessment; rising unemployment is associated with below-trend global activity captured by the CLI.
Energy prices are a major direct and indirect component of consumer price indices; rising oil prices feed through to gasoline, transportation, and production costs, raising realized CPI inflation.

Oil price changes rapidly update market participants' near-term inflation outlook; rising oil causes investors to revise long-term inflation expectations upward, increasing break-even inflation rates.
Geopolitical risk events — wars, military tensions, sanctions on oil producers — directly threaten oil supply and transit routes, causing oil price spikes as markets price in supply disruption risk.
Geopolitical risk events trigger financial market uncertainty and risk aversion, causing spikes in equity market volatility reflected in the VIX.
Geopolitical stress episodes raise uncertainty about corporate operating environments, supply chains, and counterparty risks, causing credit spreads to widen as risk premia expand.
Economic policy uncertainty raises equity market uncertainty as investors face unclear policy regimes for taxation, regulation, and trade, causing elevated implied volatility measured by VIX.

Policy uncertainty raises the risk premium demanded on corporate debt, as unclear fiscal and regulatory environments increase uncertainty about corporate cash flows and default risk, widening credit spreads.
High economic policy uncertainty raises the equity risk premium and depresses corporate investment and earnings expectations, weighing on equity returns.
Rising government debt raises concerns about future monetization and inflationary fiscal policy, pushing long-term inflation expectations embedded in break-even rates higher.
Rising fiscal debt burdens can push up the term premium and real yields as investors demand higher compensation for sovereign credit and supply risk on Treasury issuance.
Consumer pessimism about multi-year economic conditions signals reduced future consumption and corporate earnings expectations, weighing on equity prices and returns.
Consumer pessimism about future economic conditions reduces discretionary spending including luxury goods like jewelry; higher pessimism depresses consumer gold jewelry purchases.
Higher unemployment directly raises consumer anxiety about economic conditions, mechanically increasing the share of households expecting sustained bad economic times.
Rising unemployment reduces household incomes and discretionary spending power, directly reducing consumer demand for gold jewelry, especially in price-sensitive markets.
Global economic expansion raises emerging-market consumer incomes and purchasing power, boosting jewelry demand particularly in India and China where it is culturally significant.
Higher gold prices raise the cost of jewelry for price-sensitive consumers in India, China, and other major markets, creating an inverse demand response that reduces jewelry consumption.
Rising gold prices attract momentum-driven institutional and retail investors into gold ETFs, while falling prices trigger redemptions; gold price momentum directly influences ETF flow direction.

Gold price movements lead silver through portfolio substitution and shared precious-metals investor sentiment; gold is the primary precious metals benchmark and silver often follows gold directionally.

Sustained high gold prices improve mining economics and incentivize new mine development and production expansion, though the supply response operates with long lags due to mine development timescales.

Geopolitical risk and sanctions events — such as the freezing of Russian FX reserves in 2022 — directly incentivize central banks to accumulate gold as a reserve asset outside the dollar system, driving net purchases.
Spikes in financial market stress (VIX) trigger institutional safe-haven flows into gold ETFs; elevated VIX is a direct driver of positive gold ETF inflows as investors seek defensive positioning.
Rising real yields increase the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding gold ETFs, causing institutional outflows from gold ETFs as the relative attractiveness of TIPS improves.

Both silver and oil are industrial commodities with prices sensitive to global economic activity and industrial demand; common macro drivers such as global manufacturing cycles create co-movement between them.
Global economic activity is the primary driver of oil demand; a strengthening CLI signals rising industrial production and energy consumption, supporting higher oil prices.
Silver has significant industrial demand (electronics, solar panels, EVs); a rising global CLI signals stronger industrial output and manufacturing, increasing silver demand and prices.

Rising unemployment signals deteriorating economic conditions and higher default risk, causing credit spreads to widen as investors price in greater corporate distress and reduced debt-service capacity.

Realized inflation affects the real yield through its impact on monetary policy expectations and on the nominal-real decomposition of Treasury yields; higher inflation can reduce ex-post real yields and shift TIPS pricing.

Geopolitical risk events and sanctions threats directly incentivize central banks — particularly in emerging markets — to diversify reserves into gold away from dollar-denominated assets vulnerable to sanctions.

High economic policy uncertainty drives institutional investors toward defensive gold ETF positions as gold offers no exposure to policy risk, generating positive ETF inflows during uncertainty spikes.

High and rising government debt levels create uncertainty about future fiscal policy paths — potential tax increases, spending cuts, or monetization — contributing to elevated economic policy uncertainty.
